name: Panchajanya
description: Panchajanya is the divine conch shell of the Hindu god Vishnu. It is one of Vishnu's four attributes, the others being the Sudarshana Chakra, the Kaumodaki gada (mace), and Padma (lotus flower). The Panchajanya is believed to have originated from the depths of the ocean and is often depicted with a distinctive spiral shape. Its sound is considered auspicious and is said to strike fear into the hearts of enemies. In the Mahabharata, Lord Krishna, an avatar of Vishnu, blew the Panchajanya to announce the start of the Kurukshetra War. The conch's resonating sound is not just a call to battle but also a symbol of victory, righteousness, and the triumph of good over evil. It represents the five elements (earth, water, fire, air, and ether) and is considered a powerful weapon of cosmic proportions. The blowing of the Panchajanya is a recurring motif in Hindu epics and religious ceremonies, signifying the presence of the divine and the invocation of blessings. The shell holds immense spiritual significance, representing the sound of creation itself – the Om.  It is also believed that the conch was once held by the demon Panchajana, from whom Krishna rescued it after slaying the demon.
subtitle: Vishnu's divine conch shell of victory
origin: From the depths of the ocean; rescued from the demon Panchajana by Krishna.
currentLocation: Held by Lord Vishnu; symbolically present in temples and depictions of Vishnu.

keyMyths.title: Kurukshetra War Announcement
keyMyths.description: Krishna blew the Panchajanya at the start of the Kurukshetra War, signaling the beginning of the battle and filling the hearts of the Pandava army with courage. The sound instilled fear in the Kaurava forces. This act marked the commencement of a crucial event in Hindu mythology.

keyMyths.title: Slaying of Panchajana
keyMyths.description: Krishna slew the demon Panchajana and retrieved the conch shell from him, thus acquiring the Panchajanya. This act signifies Krishna's power and the vanquishing of evil to obtain a divine artifact. The conch became a symbol of Krishna's triumph over adversity.

sources.author: Veda Vyasa
sources.description: The epic Hindu text where the Panchajanya is frequently mentioned.
sources.text: The epic Hindu text where the Panchajanya is frequently mentioned.

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.description: Various Puranas contain stories and descriptions related to Vishnu and his attributes, including the Panchajanya.
